did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a feedforward backpropagation neural network to classify skin lesions (common moles, atypical nevi, and melanoma) based on ABCD features extracted via computer vision techniques (Mumford-Shah model, Harris-Stephens algorithm, HSV conversion) from dermoscopy images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a computer vision algorithm using a feedforward backpropagation neural network to classify skin lesions. It details specific methodology (ABCD feature extraction, Mumford-Shah model, Harris-Stephens algorithm), describes the dataset used (PH2 database with 200 images), and reports original results (97.51% performance). It is not a review or survey of prior literature.

title: Detection of skin cancer “Melanoma” through Computer Vision.
brief_summary: This study developed a computer vision system to detect melanoma by classifying skin lesions into common moles, atypical nevi, and melanomas using the ABCD (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter) criteria. The researchers utilized the PH2 database, which contained 200 dermoscopy images, to extract features via the Mumford-Shah model and Harris-Stephens algorithm. A feedforward backpropagation neural network with four inputs and one hidden layer was trained on 30 images to perform the classification. The system was evaluated on 201 images, including those from the database and one from the internet, achieving a performance efficiency of 97.51%. The results indicate that the proposed algorithm outperforms the typical diagnostic accuracy range of 75% to 84% reported for dermatologists.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as benign; 80 images in the PH2 database; characterized by symmetry, round/oval shape, smooth surface, defined edge, uniform color, and diameter < 6mm.

Classified as benign/pre-malignant; 80 images in the PH2 database; also known as dysplastic nevus; characterized by larger size, different color/surface/edges, mixture of colors, flat surface, jagged edge, and diameter > 5mm.

Classified as malignant; 40 images in the PH2 database (41 in results table); characterized by asymmetry, irregular/jagged/blurred borders, varied colors (red, white, black, brown), and diameter > 6mm.

all_task_types.target: Lesion category (common mole, atypical nevus, or melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a system that classifies skin lesions into three discrete diagnostic categories: common moles, atypical nevi (not common), and melanoma. The model uses a feedforward backpropagation neural network with four inputs (ABCD features) and three types of output corresponding to these classes. The performance is reported as classification efficiency (97.51%).

datasets_used.name: PH2 Database
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopy image database from the ADDI (Automatic computer-based diagnosis system for Dermoscopy Images) multidisciplinary project involving the University of Porto, University of Aveiro, and the Technical Higher Institute of Portugal. It contains 200 dermoscopy images of moles with a resolution of 768x560 pixels, BMP format, 8-bit RGB, and 20x magnification.

use.training: 30 images were used for training the neural network (10 images from each class: common, not common, and melanoma).
use.testing: The full database of 200 images was used for testing/evaluation of the system's performance.
use.other: One additional image obtained from the internet was also used for testing, bringing the total tested images to 201.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reported classification performance efficiency (97.51%) and compared it qualitatively to the typical diagnostic accuracy range of dermatologists (75% to 84%).

PH2 Database + Internet Image.notes: The system was tested on 201 images total: 200 from the PH2 database and 1 from the internet. Table I details the counts: Common mole (80 real, 79 processed), No-common (80 real, 78 processed), Melanoma (41 real, 39 processed). Total correct classifications were 196 out of 201. The paper notes that the internet image test was positive for asymmetry, border, and color, but the diameter was incorrect due to higher resolution. The system's efficiency (97.51%) is compared to the typical diagnostic accuracy range of dermatologists (75% to 84%).

architecture_used.input_size: 4 inputs (ABCD features: Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter)

architecture_details.hidden_layers: One hidden layer with fifty neurons
architecture_details.output_layer: One output layer with a neuron
architecture_details.output_classes: Three types of output (common, no-common, and melanoma)

training_details.training_samples: 30 images (10 from each class: common, not common, and melanoma)

preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of RGB images to HSV (Hue, Saturation, Value) color space for color analysis
Comparison of HSV and grayscale results to determine the prevailing color
Pre-processing fill of 'holes' in the image
Elimination of negligible areas in the image
Application of the Mumford-Shah model to detect area, center, and diameter (segmentation/edge detection)
Application of the Harris-Stephens algorithm to detect edges and corners
Conversion of pixel diameter to millimeters using a magnification factor of 20x and a pixel-to-mm ratio of 0.2645
conclusions: The developed computer vision system, which extracts ABCD (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter) features and uses a feedforward backpropagation neural network, can serve as a reliable instrument to provide a second opinion for melanoma diagnosis.
The system achieved a classification efficiency of 97.51% when tested on 201 images (200 from the PH2 database and 1 from the internet).
The system's performance (97.51%) exceeds the typical diagnostic accuracy range of dermatologists, which is reported to be between 75% and 84%.
The system provides timely and efficient results due to the rapid analysis and image processing capabilities.
The algorithm relies on the ABCD criteria; it omits the 'Evolution' parameter because the static image database lacks temporal data, requiring doctors to monitor evolution over time.

The system's diameter measurement can be inaccurate when processing images with resolutions different from the training database (e.g., internet images with higher resolution), as the conversion factor is fixed based on the 20x magnification of the PH2 database.
important_remarks: The neural network was trained on only 30 images (10 per class), which is an extremely small dataset for training a classifier, raising significant concerns about overfitting and generalizability.

The system was tested on the entire PH2 database (200 images) plus one external image, meaning the training and testing data overlap significantly (data leakage), invalidating the performance estimate.
The study omits the 'Evolution' parameter from the ABCDE criteria because the static image database lacks temporal data, limiting its clinical applicability for monitoring lesion changes over time.

Diameter measurement accuracy is dependent on the specific magnification (20x) and resolution of the PH2 database; the system fails to correctly calculate diameter for images with different resolutions (e.g., from the internet).

The reported performance (97.51%) is compared qualitatively to the typical diagnostic accuracy of dermatologists (75-84%) without statistical significance testing or a direct head-to-head clinical trial.
The initial attempt using a perceptron network failed, necessitating the switch to a feedforward backpropagation network, indicating sensitivity to model architecture choice.
The paper does not report standard classification metrics such as sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, or confusion matrix breakdowns per class, only overall efficiency.
